posts.name: Brian Pinci
posts.cooked:
I signed up for this webinar hosted by Lisa Perrotti-Brown. I was a bit worried I’d get the wines in time, they arrived one day before the event. I’ve attended quite a few on line wine tastings and she was by far the most informative host. She provided an in depth history of the Chateau, the appellations, the vintage and the wines. My partner, not a wine geek, overheard her and remarked how insightful she was. All the wines were surprisingly drinking well and quite open. I popped and poured for the event but came back to them three hours later.
Chateau Pichon Baron - a bruiser of a wine. Big and expressive. Lots of dark fruits, cedar, and leather notes. Glad I already own.
Chateau Leoville Las Cases - while very good it wasn’t quite to my liking. I kept getting whiffs of green pepper which I hate. Not a bottle I’d rush out and buy
Chateau Palmer - I had two favorites, this was one of them. The perfume on this wine was heavenly and quite floral for a Bordeaux. So much power on the palate showing rose petal, cedar, blackberry, and hints of licorice.
Chateau Pape Clement - deserving of the high rating WA gave it. A little closed but opened up later to show enormous fruit. Definitely sit on this one
Chateau Figeac - my least favorite. I couldn’t coax much out of it even after three hours. A bit thin I thought
Chateau Trotanoy - along with the Palmer, a favorite. So interesting to compare against the Palmer to see the differences in the winemaking styles as well as the grape varieties. Not as big of a nose but a huge mouthful of flavor; cigar, lavender, bark, very exotic.

Did anyone else attend the event and what are your thoughts?

posts.name: Ed Steinway
posts.cooked:
Nice notes, Brian. Appreciate you posting these!

Ed

posts.name: Rafael Aguilar
posts.cooked: Agree. Great notes, Brian! I also had the pleasure of attending this virtual tasting last night and found Lisa’s presentation to be clear, concise and insightful. The Palmer was my favorite wine of this tasting, followed by the Pichon Baron and the Leoville in that order. Would love to revisit these in 5 to 10 years. Cheers.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Sounds cool, how did it work? Did they send you sample size bottles? Cost?

posts.cooked:
Sounds cool, how did it work? Did they send you sample size bottles? Cost?

Robert beat me to the question again but I forgot to add cost?

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: Tasting kit with small bottles. $260/$295 (member/non-member).

posts.name: Brian Pinci
posts.cooked:
Sounds cool, how did it work? Did they send you sample size bottles? Cost?

It cost $260. They ship them in 50 ml bottles. She explained they get full bottles directly from the Chateau and transfer them into these smaller tasting bottles. She indicated they do it so no oxidation occurs. Not sure how that happens. And they are hermetically sealed. I don’t think you have to be a subscriber to their newsletter because I’ve seen retailers like Wine Cellarage selling it. Last month they did a Rhone tasting with Mssrs. Guigal and Chapoutier that was quite good - 12 of their top wines.

Seems totally worth it.

Fu does this trick with the little bottles. He hosted a tasting for a handful of us last year during Covid with a Levet horizontal going back to 2005. The wines were perfect.

posts.name: Julian Davies
posts.cooked:
Tasting kit with small bottles. $260/$295 (member/non-member).

Six 1.75oz pours for $260 seems …steep.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
Tasting kit with small bottles. $260/$295 (member/non-member).

Six 1.75oz pours for $260 seems …steep.

Does it? Seems not bad to me. Each of those bottles currently retails for $250-350. Presumably they’re tasting before sending out to ensure they’re correct, and so for 300ml of wine total, you’re paying about 2x retail for the privilege of tasting top-tier 2010 broadly. I’d do it just to see how the PB and Figeac are coming along, as I’ve got both but have no plans to crack a bottle for at least another 5 years.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
Tasting kit with small bottles. $260/$295 (member/non-member).

Six 1.75oz pours for $260 seems …steep.

Would agree a money maker but if the education which sounds like it was it solid I get it.

I have seen local wine educators in Minneapolis charging $50 just to talk about wine and their suggestions at the local wine shop are terrible.

posts.cooked: Sounds awesome - great notes. Looking forward to the PB in a few years. Wish they could make these work in Canada .

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I would happily have shelled out the money for it. There was a fair amount of work involved putting the wine into the small bottles, some waste, and time spent organizing etc.
I have many of the wines and the opportunity to taste to see where they are and perhaps find wine I hdon’t have makes sense.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Fu does this trick with the little bottles. He hosted a tasting for a handful of us last year during Covid with a Levet horizontal going back to 2005. The wines were perfect.

So a week of shipping after exposure to air really helped these, eh?

And Brian, cool notes, thanks for reporting

In our case, the wines are professionally rebottled under inert gas in a Bordeaux facility that specializes in this. I’ve compared the tubes vs bottles from my own cellar and the wines are, impressively, identically.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I’ve taken part in this sort of virtual tasting several times now over the last year, organized by a French wine merchant/auction house.

I personally think this development is one of the good things to have emerged during times of Covid. I live about 45 minutes from Paris, so going to wine tastings in the evenings is difficult because of the question of transport, especially going home. This new idea allows me to taste stuff I’m interested in without buying whole bottles.

So far, it has enabled me to get into Belargus Savennières and Anjou Blanc, to confirm that I like some Camin Larredya Jurançons but not all, that I don’t like many Rhones and Beaujolais and saved me a lot of money by showing me that Sylvain Pataille’s Marsannays were definitely not for me! Overall I reckon I saved about 90€ after counting the cost of the tasting kits.

I don’t find $260 for six samples excessive at all, if like me it saves you money in the process, whether you take part in the tutorials or not.
